elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we provide the following simple equivalent condition for a nonsymmetric Algebraic Riccati Equation to admit a stabilizing cone-preserving solution: an associated coefficient matrix must be stable.
elements.sent-2411-002: The result holds under the assumption that said matrix be cross-positive on a proper cone, and it both extends and completes a corresponding sufficient condition for nonnegative matrices in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-003: Further, key to showing the above is the following result which we also provide: in order for a monotonically increasing sequence of cone-preserving matrices to converge, it is sufficient to be bounded above in a single vectorial direction.
elements.sent-2411-004: Algebraic Riccati equations have been studied extensively in the literature over the years, e.g., \cite{lancaster1995algebraic and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-005: They often appear on the form \begin{equation}XBX + DX + XA + C = 0,\end{equation} where $A, B, C, D \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$, and are not only of theoretical but also of practical interest.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Often, as in control theory \cite{dullerud2013course}\cite{willems1971least , $D$ is taken as $A^T$ with $B, C$ symmetric, and a symmetric solution $X$ is sought for.}
elements.sent-2411-007: A typical result under the assumption of stabilizability may provide a unique stabilizing symmetric solution \cite{willems1971least .}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, more recently there has been an increased interest in its nonsymmetric counterpart, see e.g., \cite{freiling2002survey and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-009: The specific case in which the negative of the matrix \begin{equation}L =  A B C D \end{equation} forms an M-matrix, i.e., an anti-stable matrix with nonpositive offdiagonal elements, with applications in transport theory and Markov models, has attracted some interest, see e.g., \cite{guo2001nonsymmetric}\cite{guo2007iterative and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In particular, part of \cite[Theorem 1.1]{guo2007iterative provides the following sufficient condition for the existence of a stabilizing, entrywise nonnegative solution: $-L$ should be an M-matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Analogue statements for irreducible singular matrices and more recently also regular matrices \cite{guo2016algebraic are known to hold.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The objective of the present paper is to both generalize the above result to proper cones as well as to complete it into an equivalence (Theorem \ref{thrm:cones ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: Subsequent papers on the topic appear to be focused exclusively on the M-matrix case and its applications, with no mention about proper cones to the best of the authors' knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-014: By contrast, the main purpose of this paper is the desire to better understand the result by identifying the structure which generates it: the conic structure of the nonnegative orthant in $\mathbb{R ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition, the main result turns out to be useful also in a different context, namely control theory \cite{vladu2024selfdual .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In order to prove the main result, a fixed-point iteration approach similar to the one in \cite{guo2001nonsymmetric is used in one direction.}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, rather than exploiting the Kronecker product, we shall pass through the analytical integral solution of the Sylvester equation.
elements.sent-2411-018: Further, and more importantly, convergence no longer becomes a simple matter of applying the monotone convergence theorem elementwise.
elements.sent-2411-019: As a result, we also provide a somewhat unexpected convergence result (Theorem \ref{thrm:mono ) for a sequence of monotonically increasing cone-preserving matrices with a vectorial upper bound.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This is novel to the best of the authors' knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-021: The outline of the paper is as follows: Section 2 reviews the crucial notion of cross-positivity on a proper cone as well as some elementary facts in cone theory.
elements.sent-2411-022: In Section 3, we present the results of the paper along with their proofs.
elements.sent-2411-023: Section 4 subsequently concludes the paper with some suggestions for future works.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this section, we clarify the notation used in the paper and provide the necessary background required for the results.
elements.sent-2411-025: In this subsection, we explain some definitions and notation used throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-026: If $A \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$, then $A$ is said to be stable if all its eigenvalues have negative real part.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In this subsection, we provide some necessary background on cone theory from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-028: For more results on this topic, see e.g., \cite{berman1994nonnegative}\cite{schneider2006matrices and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-029: A set $K \subseteq \mathbb{R ^n$ is said to be a cone if $x \in K$ and $\alpha \geq 0$ imply $\alpha x \in K$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: A convex, closed and pointed ($K \cap -K = \{0\}$) cone with non-empty interior is said to be proper.
elements.sent-2411-031: A proper cone $K$ induces a partial order $\succeq_K$ such that $x \succeq_K y$ if and only if $x - y \in K$.
elements.sent-2411-032: If $x - y$ lies in the interior of $K$, we say that $x \succ_K y$.
elements.sent-2411-033: The standard example of a proper cone is the nonnegative orthant in $\mathbb{R ^n$, and $\succeq_K$ then reduces to the usual inequality between real numbers applied entrywise.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Given a proper cone $K \subseteq \mathbb{R ^n$, the associated dual cone is defined as \begin{equation}K_* = \{y \in R^n \mid y^T x \geq 0 \; for \; all \; x \in K\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}K_* = \{y \in R^n \mid y^T x \geq 0 \; for \; all \; x \in K\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: The interior of the dual cone consists of all those $y$ such that $y^T x > 0$ for all nonzero $x \in K$.
elements.sent-2411-036: If a cone is proper, the associated dual cone is also proper \cite[Chap.. 2.6.1]{boyd2004convex .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Further, it is routine to verify from the definitions that $\big( K \times K \big)_* = K_* \times K_*$ and that $K \times K$ is a proper cone in $\mathbb{R ^{2n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: A matrix $A \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ is said to be cross-positive on $K$ if $x \in K$ and $y \in K_*$ with $y^T x = 0$ imply $y^T A x \geq 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, the set of cross-positive matrices on the nonnegative orthant is simply the set of matrices with nonnegative offdiagonal elements.
elements.sent-2411-040: For more on cross-positive matrices, see e.g., \cite{schneider1970cross .}
elements.sent-2411-041: A matrix $A \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ such that $AK \subseteq K$ for a proper cone $K \subseteq \mathbb{R ^n$ is said to be $K$-nonnegative or leave $K$ invariant.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The set of such matrices is denoted $\pi (K)$ and is itself known to be a proper cone in $\mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ \cite[Chap.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Hence, if a proper cone $K \subseteq \mathbb{R ^n$ is specified, $X \succeq_K Y$ where $X, Y \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ means that $X - Y \in \pi (K)$, i.e., $X - Y$ is $K$-nonnegative.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Next, we gather some elementary facts about $K$-nonnegativity and cross-positivity of which we shall make frequent use.
elements.sent-2411-045: Given $x, y \in \mathbb{R ^n$, $A, B \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$ and a proper cone $K \subseteq \mathbb{R ^n$, the following holds:.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: If $A$ and $B$ are $K$-nonnegative, then so is $A + B$ and $AB$.
elements.sent-2411-047: If $A$ is $K$-nonnegative and $x \succeq_K y$, then $Ax \succeq_K Ay$.
elements.sent-2411-049: If $A$ is $K$-nonnegative, then $A$ is cross-positive on $K$.
elements.sent-2411-050: If $A$ and $B$ are cross-positive on $K$, then so is $A + B$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Regarding (i), if $x \in K$, then $(A + B)x = Ax + Bx \succeq_K 0$ and $ABx = Ay \succeq_K 0$ with $y = Bx \succeq_K 0$ since $K$ is a proper cone.
elements.sent-2411-052: As for (ii), note that $Ax - Ay = A(x - y) \succeq_K 0$ so that $Ax \succeq_K Ay$, since $x - y \in K$ by assumption.
elements.sent-2411-053: Similarly, in (iii) we note that $A - B$ is $K$-nonnegative by assumption, and so $(A - B)x \succeq_K 0$, i.e., $Ax \succeq_K Bx$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Further, (iv) follows by definition of cross-positivity, as $z = Ax \succeq_K 0$ if $x \succeq_K 0$ by assumption, so that $y^T Ax = y^T z \geq 0$ if $y \in K_*$ by definition of the dual cone.
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, if $x \in K$, $y \in K_*$ with $y^T x = 0$, we have $y^T(A + B)x = y^T A x + y^T Bx \geq 0$ by assumption and so $A + B$ is cross-positive, i.e., (v) holds.

elements.sent-2411-135: Altogether, we have shown that $0 = X_0 \preceq_K X_i \preceq_K X_{i + 1}$ and that there exist $r, s \in \mathbb{R ^{n}$ with $r \succ_K 0$ such that $X_i r \preceq_K s$ for all $i \geq 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-136: An application of Theorem \ref{thrm:mono thus shows that $\{X_i \}_{i = 1 ^\infty$ is convergent, i.e., $X_i \rightarrow X_*$ as $i \rightarrow \infty$ for some $X_* \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Consequently, since the sequence satisfies (\ref{eq:rec ), we have \begin{equation}X_* BX_* + DX_* + X_* A + C = 0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Further, since $\pi (K)$ is a proper cone and hence closed, see Section 2, $\{X_i \}_{i = 1 ^\infty$ converges inside $\pi (K)$, i.e., $X_* \succeq_K 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-139: It remains to show that $A + BX_*$ and $D + X_* B$ are stable and cross-positive on $K$.
elements.sent-2411-140: For this purpose, note that $X_* v_1 \preceq_K v_2$ because $K$ is closed.
elements.sent-2411-141: Together with the assumption $B \succeq_K 0$ and Proposition \ref{prop:basic (ii), we thus have \begin{equation}(A + BX_*)v_1 \preceq_K Av_1 + Bv_2 = u_1 \prec 0,\end{equation} where in the final equality the first expression in (\ref{eq:Lv ) was invoked.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: But $A + BX_*$ is cross-positive on $K$ according to Proposition \ref{prop:basic (i), (iv) and (v), since $A$ is also cross-positive on $K$ by assumption.}
elements.sent-2411-143: It thus follows from Lemma \ref{lem:cross_stable that $A + BX_*$ is stable.}
elements.sent-2411-144: As for $D + X_* B$, note that the matrix \begin{equation} D^T B^T C^T A^T  =  0 I I 0   A B C D ^T  0 I I 0 \end{equation} has the same spectrum as $L$ and is therefore stable, as it is a similarity transform of $L^T$.

elements.sent-2411-146: By the part of Theorem \ref{thrm:cones proved so far, the equation \begin{equation}ZB^T Z + A^T Z + ZD^T + C^T = 0\end{equation} must therefore have a solution $Z_* \succeq_{K_* 0$ such that $D^T + B^T Z_*$ is stable and cross-positive on $K_*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Additionally, such a solution can be taken as the limit of a sequence $\{Z_i\}_{i=0 ^{\infty}$ with $Z_0 = 0$ which satisfies the corresponding recursion (\ref{eq:rec ), i.e., \begin{equation}A^T Z_{i + 1} + Z_{i + 1}D^T = -Z_i B^T Z_i - C^T .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: But according to first part of the proof, this recursion is also satisfied by $\{X_i^T\}_{i = 0 ^{\infty}$ with $X_0 = 0$ once (\ref{eq:rec ) is transposed.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Because the sequence generated by the recursion was shown to be unique, we may conclude that $Z_i = X_i ^T$ and so by the continuity of the transpose operator $Z_* = X_* ^T$.
elements.sent-2411-150: But then the stability and cross-positivity of $D^T + B^T Z_* = D^T + B^T X_* ^T$ on $K_*$ implies the stability and cross-positivity of $D + X_* B = (D^T + B^T X_* ^T) ^T$ on $K$.
elements.sent-2411-151: This concludes the sufficiency part of the proof.
elements.sent-2411-152: As for necessity, note first that by the following well-known similarity transformation we have \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \begin{pmatrix} I 0 -X_* I \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} A B -C -D \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} I 0 X_* I \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} A + BX_* B -X_* BX_* - DX_* - X_* A - C -(D + X_* B) \end{pmatrix}\end{aligned}\end{equation} Consequently, the assumptions yield \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} -L^{-1} = - \Bigg( \begin{pmatrix} I 0 0 -I \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} A B -C -D \end{pmatrix} \Bigg)^{-1} = - \Bigg( \begin{pmatrix} I 0 0 -I \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} I 0 X_* I \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} A + BX_* B 0 -(D + X_* B) \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} I 0 -X_* I \end{pmatrix} \Bigg)^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} I 0 X_* I \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} PX_* -(A + BX_* )^{-1} P -(D + X_* B)^{-1} X_* -(D + X_* B)^{-1} \end{pmatrix} \succeq_{K \times K} 0 \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $P = (A + BX_* )^{-1 B (D + X_* B)^{-1}$.}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}  I 0 -X_* I   A B -C -D   I 0 X_* I  =  A + BX_* B -X_* BX_* - DX_* - X_* A - C -(D + X_* B) \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} -L^{-1} = - \Bigg(  I 0 0 -I   A B -C -D  \Bigg)^{-1} = - \Bigg(  I 0 0 -I   I 0 X_* I   A + BX_* B 0 -(D + X_* B)   I 0 -X_* I  \Bigg)^{-1} =  I 0 X_* I   PX_* -(A + BX_* )^{-1} P -(D + X_* B)^{-1} X_* -(D + X_* B)^{-1}  \succeq_{K \times K} 0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-153: Here, the fact that $X_*$ solves $XBX + DX + XA + C = 0$ was invoked in the second equality.

elements.sent-2411-155: Further, it is clear that if all four matrices in a $2 \times 2$ block matrix are $K$-nonnegative, then the block matrix is $K \times K$-nonnegative.
elements.sent-2411-156: As such, since also $X_* \succeq_K 0$ by assumption, the final inequality follows.
elements.sent-2411-157: But since it is assumed that $L$ is cross-positive on $K \times K$, another application of Lemma \ref{lem:cross_stable implies that $L$ is stable.}
elements.sent-2411-159: In this paper, the following equivalent condition is supplied for a nonsymmetric algebraic Riccati equation to admit a stabilizing cone-preserving solution: an associated coefficient matrix should be stable.
elements.sent-2411-160: This extends and completes an already published sufficient condition on the nonnegative orthant into an equivalence for general proper cones.
elements.sent-2411-161: Many additional properties, such as the minimality of the cone-preserving solution, follow from the stability of the aforementioned coefficient matrix in the well-studied nonnegative case.
elements.sent-2411-162: While this lies beyond the scope of the present paper, establishing how well these features generalize to proper cones would be interesting for future works.
elements.sent-2411-163: The authors are grateful to Dr. Dongjun Wu for proofreading the manuscript and providing helpful comments.
elements.sent-2411-164: A typical result under the assumption of stabilizability and detectability may provide a unique stabilizing solution in certain settings, e.g., \cite{kuvcera1973review .}
elements.sent-2411-165: In addition, the main result turns out to be useful also in a different context, namely control theory \cite{vladu2024stability .}
